sections.header: Verses 1–9 — "Everything Is Loss Compared to Knowing Christ"

heb.paragraph: The noun skybala (v. 8) is the crudest term Paul uses in his letters. It denotes garbage, table scraps, or excrement—worthless refuse fit only for the dung heap. Paul applies it to his most prized accomplishments: circumcision, tribal pedigree, Pharisaic zeal, law-righteousness. The shock is deliberate: what Judaism values most highly, Paul regards as waste compared to knowing Christ.

Paul contrasts two kinds of righteousness in v. 9: ‘my own righteousness that comes from the law’ (emēn dikaiosynēn tēn ek nomou) and ‘the righteousness that comes from God on the basis of faith’ (tēn ek theou dikaiosynēn epi tē pistei). The first is self-generated through Torah performance; the second is God-given through faith. The contrast echoes Gal 2:16 and Rom 3:21–22.

refs.note: Paul’s pre-conversion credentials in Galatians parallel Phil 3:4–6, with both passages counting Jewish accomplishments as loss compared to Christ.
Paul’s lament that Israel pursued righteousness by works rather than by faith provides the theological framework for the law/faith contrast in Phil 3:9.

notes.note: The warning against ‘dogs’ reverses the Jewish epithet for Gentiles: the true ‘dogs’ are those who mutilate the flesh (circumcision advocates). The ‘true circumcision’ (v. 3) consists of those who worship by the Spirit, glory in Christ, and put no confidence in the flesh.

Paul’s credentials are impeccable by any Jewish standard: born into the covenant, zealous for the law, blameless in observance. MacArthur notes that Paul is not attacking a straw man but the best case for law-righteousness—his own.

The accounting metaphor is decisive: what was ‘profit’ is now ‘loss.’ The surpassing value is knowing Christ—not cognitive knowledge but relational, experiential union. The righteousness Paul now possesses is not his own but God’s, received through faith.

Calvin emphasises that Paul does not merely abandon lesser goods; he counts them as rubbish. The comparison is not between good and better but between refuse and treasure. Calvin reads this as the annihilation of all human merit: if Paul’s extraordinary righteousness is dung, no human achievement can contribute to justification. The righteousness that matters is ‘from God on the basis of faith’—entirely alien, entirely received.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The triple repetition of blepete (watch out for) creates an emphatic warning. The terms ‘dogs’ (kynas), ‘evildoers’ (kakous ergatas), and ‘mutilators’ (katātomēn, a wordplay on peritomē/circumcision) are deliberately provocative.

The verb hēgēmai (I consider) is in the present tense: Paul’s assessment is not a past decision but a continuing evaluation. The preposition dia + accusative (dia to hyperechon) indicates the reason: the surpassing greatness of knowing Christ makes everything else worthless by comparison.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, Philippians, NICNT (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee reads the polemic as directed against the same kind of Judaizing opponents Paul faced in Galatia, though the Philippian situation may be less acute. Fee emphasises that Paul’s argument is not anti-Jewish but anti-legalist: the problem is not the law itself but law-observance as the basis of right standing. The experiential language of v. 8 (‘knowing Christ Jesus my Lord’) is Pentecostal in flavour: knowledge of Christ is relational and transformative, not merely doctrinal.

silva.source: Moisés Silva, Philippians, BECNT (2nd ed., 2005) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Silva identifies vv. 2–9 as a carefully structured argument: warning (v. 2), thesis (v. 3), credentials (vv. 4–6), reversal (vv. 7–9). The key term is hēgeomai (consider, regard): Paul’s changed evaluation of his credentials reflects a fundamental reorientation of values. Silva notes that the phrase ‘to know Christ’ (v. 8) echoes the relational knowledge of the OT prophets (Jer 9:24; Hos 6:6).

hist.context: Philippians 3 shifts abruptly from the warm tone of ch. 2 to sharp polemic. Paul warns against ‘dogs’ and ‘mutilators of the flesh’ (v. 2)—Judaizing opponents who promote circumcision. He then catalogues his own Jewish credentials (vv. 4–6)—circumcised on the eighth day, of the tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew of Hebrews, a Pharisee, a persecutor of the church, faultless regarding legalistic righteousness—only to declare them all ‘loss’ (zēmia) compared to the surpassing worth of knowing Christ (vv. 7–8). The autobiographical catalogue mirrors Gal 1:13–14 and functions identically: Paul’s credentials exceed those of the Judaizers, making his rejection of law-righteousness all the more devastating.

sections.header: Verses 10–14 — "I Press On toward the Goal"

heb.paragraph: The verb diōkō (vv. 12, 14) carries the athletic connotation of a runner straining toward the finish line. Paul uses it three times to express his relentless pursuit of Christ-likeness. The aorist passive kategnōsthn (v. 12, ‘I was taken hold of’) establishes the priority: Christ first seized Paul; now Paul pursues the goal for which he was seized.

refs.note: Paul’s statement about being ‘united with him in a death like his’ to be ‘united with him in a resurrection like his’ parallels the death-resurrection sequence in Phil 3:10–11.
Paul’s athletic metaphor—running to win the prize, disciplining the body—provides the closest parallel to the race imagery of Phil 3:12–14.

notes.note: The knowledge Paul pursues is not informational but participatory: he wants to experience resurrection power operating through suffering. MacArthur notes the paradoxical sequence: power is manifested in weakness, life through death. The phrase ‘somehow, to attain’ (v. 11) does not express doubt about the resurrection but humility before its magnitude.

Paul’s disclaimer of perfection (‘not that I have already obtained’) demolishes perfectionism while affirming progressive sanctification. The athletic metaphor demands total concentration: ‘forgetting what is behind’ includes both past failures and past achievements. The prize is not salvation (already secured) but the full apprehension of Christ for which Christ apprehended Paul.

Calvin reads Paul’s disclaimer as a corrective to the complacency that could follow from the assurance of election. The believer is simultaneously secure in Christ and actively pursuing Christ-likeness. Calvin insists that ‘forgetting what is behind’ applies to achievements as much as to failures: past spiritual progress is no ground for rest.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb katalamβanō (to take hold of, lay hold of) appears twice: Paul pursues to lay hold of that for which Christ ‘laid hold of’ him. The aorist passive katelēmphthēn establishes divine initiative: Christ’s seizing of Paul on the Damascus road is the ground of Paul’s pursuit.

The noun brabeion (prize) is an athletic term for the winner’s award at the games. The compound phrase tēs anō klēseōs (of the upward calling) may mean ‘heavenly calling’ or ‘the calling that draws upward.’

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, Philippians, NICNT (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee identifies the tension between the ‘already’ (knowing Christ, being found in him) and the ‘not yet’ (attaining the resurrection, winning the prize) as the animating force of Paul’s spirituality. Fee reads the suffering motif as participatory rather than merely imitational: believers share in Christ’s sufferings through the Spirit’s work, not through moral imitation alone.

silva.source: Moisés Silva, Philippians, BECNT (2nd ed., 2005) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Silva reads vv. 10–14 as the positive counterpart to vv. 4–9: having rejected law-righteousness, Paul describes the content of faith-righteousness—experiential knowledge of Christ through participation in his death-resurrection pattern. Silva notes the eschatological tension: Paul has not yet attained (v. 12) but is confident of attaining (v. 11) because Christ has already seized him. The already-not yet dynamic governs the believer’s ethical effort.

hist.context: Paul’s desire to know Christ (v. 10) is expansive: it includes ‘the power of his resurrection, participation in his sufferings, becoming like him in his death, and so, somehow, attaining to the resurrection from the dead.’ The order is significant: resurrection power comes first, but it operates through suffering and conformity to Christ’s death. Paul disclaims perfection (v. 12)—‘not that I have already obtained all this’—and adopts the posture of a runner pressing forward: ‘forgetting what is behind and straining toward what is ahead, I press on toward the goal to win the prize for which God has called me heavenward in Christ Jesus’ (vv. 13–14). The athletic metaphor conveys urgency, effort, and single-minded focus.

heb.paragraph: The noun politeuma (v. 20) denotes the place of one’s citizenship or the citizen body to which one belongs. In a Roman colony like Philippi, citizenship was a matter of civic pride and legal protection. Paul’s declaration that ‘our citizenship is in heaven’ (hēmōn gar to politeuma en ouranois hyparchei) redefines the Philippians’ primary identity: they are a colony of heaven on earth, awaiting the return of their true Sovereign.

refs.note: Paul’s detailed account of bodily transformation at the resurrection provides the fuller eschatological framework for the compressed statement in Phil 3:21.
John’s affirmation that ‘when Christ appears, we shall be like him, for we shall see him as he is’ parallels the transformation promise of Phil 3:21.

notes.note: Paul urges the Philippians to follow his example and warns against those whose end is destruction. MacArthur identifies these ‘enemies of the cross’ as professing Christians living in moral compromise: their appetites are their god, and they boast in what should shame them.

The heavenly citizenship declaration is the theological climax of the chapter. MacArthur notes the military resonance: Philippi was a Roman military colony whose citizens lived in Philippi but owed allegiance to Rome. Believers live on earth but owe allegiance to heaven. The transformation promise (v. 21) guarantees bodily resurrection and glorification.

Calvin reads the citizenship language as a counter to earthly-mindedness: believers must not become so attached to earthly concerns that they forget their heavenly identity. The transformation of the body assures believers that the present body of weakness will be exchanged for a body of glory, conformable to Christ’s own risen body.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb apekdechometha (we eagerly await) is a compound expressing intense expectation. The NET translators note that the sōtēr (Saviour) title was applied to the emperor in Roman political theology; Paul’s application to Christ is deliberately counter-imperial.

The verb metaschēmatisei (will transform) uses schēma (outward form), distinguishing from morphē (essential nature): the body’s outward condition will change, while the person’s identity remains continuous.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, Philippians, NICNT (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee reads the heavenly citizenship declaration as the passage’s theological centre. The enemies of the cross (vv. 18–19) represent the libertine opposite of the Judaizers (vv. 2–4): Paul fights on two fronts, against both legalism and licence. The transformation promise (v. 21) is eschatological and pneumatological: it is by the Spirit’s power that Christ will transform believers’ bodies.

silva.source: Moisés Silva, Philippians, BECNT (2nd ed., 2005) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Silva identifies the politeuma statement as the letter’s most politically charged declaration. In a city that prized its Roman colonial status, Paul relocates the community’s deepest allegiance to a heavenly commonwealth. Silva notes that the transformation of v. 21 completes the Christ-hymn’s trajectory: Christ descended to a body of humiliation (2:7–8); at his return, he will transform believers’ bodies of humiliation into conformity with his body of glory.

hist.context: Paul transitions from personal testimony to communal exhortation. He urges the ‘mature’ (teleioi, v. 15) to share his perspective, while acknowledging that God will clarify any remaining disagreements (v. 15b). He warns against ‘enemies of the cross of Christ’ (v. 18)—likely libertines whose ‘god is their stomach’ and whose ‘glory is in their shame’ (v. 19). The contrast with believers is stark: their citizenship is in heaven, from which they await a Saviour who will transform their lowly bodies to be like his glorious body (vv. 20–21). The ‘transformation of the body’ (metaschēmatisei) echoes the morphological language of the Christ-hymn (2:6–7) and applies it eschatologically: the same power that enabled Christ’s self-emptying and exaltation will transform believers’ bodies at the parousia.

themes.note: Faith dominates as Paul describes the exchange of law-righteousness for faith-righteousness and the pursuit of knowing Christ. Holiness is prominent through the pressing-on motif. Prophecy appears through the eschatological transformation promise.

rows.label: vv.1–3
rows.text: Warning: beware of dogs, mutilators; true circumcision

rows.label: vv.4–6
rows.text: Paul’s Jewish credentials: circumcised, Pharisee, blameless

rows.label: vv.7–9
rows.text: All is loss: rubbish compared to knowing Christ

rows.label: vv.10–11
rows.text: To know him: resurrection power, fellowship of sufferings

rows.label: vv.12–14
rows.text: Pressing on: forgetting behind, straining toward the prize

rows.label: vv.18–19
rows.text: Enemies of the cross: their god is their stomach

rows.label: vv.20–21
rows.text: Citizenship in heaven; transformation of the body

lit.note: Philippians 3 moves from polemic against Judaizers (vv. 1–9) through Paul’s personal pursuit of Christ (vv. 10–14) to the eschatological hope of heavenly citizenship and bodily transformation (vv. 15–21). The chapter’s centre is the radical exchange: law-righteousness as rubbish, Christ-righteousness as treasure.

hebtext.note: The noun skybala (v. 8) is the crudest term Paul uses in his letters. It denotes garbage, table scraps, or excrement—worthless refuse fit only for the dung heap. Paul applies it to his most prized accomplishments: circumcision, tribal pedigree, Pharisaic zeal, law-righteousness. Paul contrasts two kinds of righteousness in v. 9: ‘my own righteousness that comes from the law’ (emēn dikaiosynēn tēn ek nomou) and ‘the righteousness that comes from God on the basis of faith’ (tēn ek theou dikaiosynēn epi tē pistei). The first is self-generated through Torah performance; the second is God-given through faith. The contrast echoes Gal 2:16 and Rom 3:21–22.

The verb diōkō (vv. 12, 14) carries the athletic connotation of a runner straining toward the finish line. Paul uses it three times to express his relentless pursuit of Christ-likeness. The aorist passive kategnōsthn (v. 12, ‘I was taken hold of’) establishes the priority: Christ first seized Paul; now Paul pursues the goal for which he was seized.

The noun politeuma (v. 20) denotes the place of one’s citizenship or the citizen body to which one belongs. In a Roman colony like Philippi, citizenship was a matter of civic pride and legal protection. Paul’s declaration that ‘our citizenship is in heaven’ (hēmōn gar to politeuma en ouranois hyparchei) redefines the Philippians’ primary identity: they are a colony of heaven on earth, awaiting the return of their true Sovereign.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: 4–6)—circumcised on the eighth day, of the tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew of Hebrews, a Pharisee, a persecutor of the church, faultless regarding legalistic righteousness—only to declare them all ‘loss’ (zēmia) compared to the surpassing worth…

thread.text: Paul’s pre-conversion credentials in Galatians parallel Phil 3:4–6, with both passages counting Jewish accomplishments as loss compared to Christ.
Paul’s lament that Israel pursued righteousness by works rather than by faith provides the theological framework for the law/faith contrast in Phil 3:9.
Paul’s statement about being ‘united with him in a death like his’ to be ‘united with him in a resurrection like his’ parallels the death-resurrection sequence in Phil 3:10–11.
Paul’s athletic metaphor—running to win the prize, disciplining the body—provides the closest parallel to the race imagery of Phil 3:12–14.
Paul’s detailed account of bodily transformation at the resurrection provides the fuller eschatological framework for the compressed statement in Phil 3:21.
John’s affirmation that ‘when Christ appears, we shall be like him, for we shall see him as he is’ parallels the transformation promise of Phil 3:21.

discourse.thesis: Paul counts all as loss compared to knowing Christ — press on toward the goal for the prize.

nodes.text: Rejoice in the Lord! Beware of dogs, evil workers, those who mutilate the flesh. We are the true circumcision — we worship by the Spirit.
Paul had confidence in the flesh: circumcised, tribe of Benjamin, Pharisee, zealous, blameless under law. But he counts it all loss for the surpassing worth of knowing Christ.
Not that Paul has obtained this or is perfect — but he presses on because Christ Jesus made him his own. Forgetting what lies behind, straining forward, he presses toward the goal.
Join in imitating Paul. Many walk as enemies of the cross — their god is their belly. But our citizenship is in heaven, from which we await a Savior who will transform our lowly bodies.

coaching.tip: Paul's credentials (vv. 4-6) were impeccable: circumcised, Benjaminite, Pharisee, zealous, 'faultless' under law. Yet he considers them 'garbage' (v. 8)—skybala, refuse. Why? They competed with Christ. Impressive resumes become liabilities when they obscure the Savior.

'I press on toward the goal' (v. 14). Athletic imagery: Paul is mid-race, not resting on past victories. 'Forgetting what is behind' (v. 13) isn't amnesia but refusal to let history—good or bad—determine trajectory. The prize is ahead, not behind.
